explanation: Both claims address AI-assisted negotiation but target different dimensions. Claim A identifies a design principle (process orientation > directives) for humanitarian negotiators' tool preferences, while Claim B tests whether feedback improves actual performance and finds differential effects by agent type. These findings could coexist: a process-oriented tool design might be preferred (Claim A) while zero-shot feedback specifically fails to improve human negotiators (Claim B) — suggesting design preferences and outcome efficacy are separate concerns.
claim_a_text: Frontline humanitarian negotiators found process-oriented AI tools that surfaced options and risks more effective than tools providing direct strategy recommendations
claim_b_text: Feedback based on zero-shot GPT-4 prompting improved LLM negotiator performance in simulated bargaining but failed to improve human negotiator performance
claim_a_paper: ChatGPT, Don't Tell Me What to Do: Designing AI for Context Analysis in Humanitarian Frontline Negotiations
claim_b_paper: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System
gold_notes: Ma et al. and ACE. Both converge on the insight that what works for AI does not transfer to humans - Ma qualitatively (humans prefer process support), ACE quantitatively (LLM feedback helps LLMs not humans).
engine_resolution: Future research should test whether process-oriented AI tools (Claim A's preferred design) combined with better-tuned feedback mechanisms (addressing Claim B's failure with zero-shot prompting) improves both human preference and actual negotiation outcomes.

explanation: Both claims describe genuine psychological effects of training/coaching interventions, but they measure different constructs: Claim A tracks self-efficacy (perceived capability), while Claim B tracks anxiety/fear reduction (emotional barriers). A realistic understanding of conflict difficulty could simultaneously lower confidence while targeted AI coaching lowers emotional anxiety. These are complementary rather than opposing findings.
claim_a_text: Simulated conflict practice with Rehearsal reduced participants' conflict self-efficacy after training, as they gained a realistic understanding of how difficult conflicts are
claim_b_text: A theory-driven AI coach achieved the largest reduction in negotiation-related fear, suggesting AI coaching reduces anxiety and psychological barriers
claim_a_paper: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution
claim_b_paper: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?
gold_notes: Rehearsal vs. Trucey. Rehearsal: self-efficacy decreased after AI practice. Trucey: fear decreased after AI coaching. Opposite directional conclusions on psychological effect of AI-assisted practice.
engine_resolution: Future research should measure both constructs (self-efficacy and anxiety) in the same study to clarify whether anxiety reduction and efficacy reduction represent distinct psychological pathways or whether one moderates the other, and whether combining realistic practice with anxiety-reducing coaching produces different outcomes than either alone.

explanation: These claims describe genuinely different outcomes of theory-driven AI design. Claim A focuses on output fidelity (realistic conflict simulations), while Claim B focuses on user psychology (fear reduction). Both studies show theory-grounded approaches outperforming baselines, but on different dependent variables. Together they suggest theory-driven design benefits both the quality of AI-generated content and the emotional safety of learners.
claim_a_text: Theory-grounded LLM prompting (IRP) significantly outperforms standard instruction-following LLMs for generating realistic conflict simulations
claim_b_text: A theory-driven AI coach built on Brett and Thompson's negotiation framework produced significantly better fear reduction than a generic ChatGPT condition using the same underlying model
claim_a_paper: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution
claim_b_paper: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?
gold_notes: Rehearsal and Trucey. Both show theory grounding produces better outcomes than generic LLM use. Different mechanisms, same conclusion.
engine_resolution: Future research should measure both constructs in the same study—testing whether realistic simulations from theory-grounded prompting also improve emotional outcomes (fear reduction, confidence) in the same sample.

explanation: These claims describe genuinely orthogonal constructs. Claim A measures internal classification performance (precision/recall on category detection), while Claim B measures external validity (whether the resulting metrics predict actual negotiation outcomes). A system can be accurate at detecting dialogue features yet those features may or may not correlate with success—these are independent questions about system quality and utility.
claim_a_text: ACE achieved high error detection accuracy (above 0.90 for all categories) but lower F1 scores for qualitative categories like strategic closing (F1=0.54)
claim_b_text: Automatic metrics derived from negotiation dialogue annotations significantly correlated with negotiation outcomes including initial claim (r=0.47) and agreement time (r=0.59)
claim_a_paper: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System
claim_b_paper: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills
gold_notes: ACE and Johnson et al. Both validate automated negotiation assessment against ground truth using different approaches. Both support feasibility.
engine_resolution: Future research should explicitly test whether the detection system used in Claim A (high accuracy, lower F1 for qualitative categories) produces the same metrics studied in Claim B, to determine whether detection errors propagate into weakened outcome correlations.

explanation: These claims make opposing assertions about the same core question: whether feedback is necessary for negotiation improvement. Claim A treats feedback as a prerequisite; Claim B demonstrates improvement without it. Both cannot be simultaneously true in their strongest form, though the contradiction could be resolved by scope (e.g., feedback helps some learners but not others, or certain agent designs eliminate the need for explicit feedback).
claim_a_text: People do not learn negotiation tactics simply from the experience of bargaining; they require structured feedback and instruction to improve
claim_b_text: Even without feedback, autonomous agent systems improve negotiation ability, and people are more comfortable learning from automated agents than from other people
claim_a_paper: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System
claim_b_paper: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills
gold_notes: ACE: experience alone insufficient without feedback. Johnson: practice with agents helps even without feedback. Differ in degree not direction - some improvement is not the same as sufficiency.
engine_resolution: Future research should directly test whether autonomous agent interaction (without explicit feedback) produces equivalent learning gains to structured coaching with feedback, controlling for agent quality, learner experience level, and task complexity.

explanation: Both claims are about AI negotiation coaching, but they address genuinely different constructs that can coexist. Claim A examines a technical mechanism within one system; Claim B examines individual-level moderation of coaching outcomes. Together they suggest that AI coaching design features and user demographic factors are both important determinants of negotiation AI effectiveness.
claim_a_text: The ACE negotiation agent uses dynamic prompting with an adjustable subjective limit to avoid being a pushover and to give robust counteroffers
claim_b_text: Cultural background significantly moderated the effectiveness of AI negotiation coaching across participant groups
claim_a_paper: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System
claim_b_paper: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?
gold_notes: ACE prompting mechanism (method) vs. Trucey cultural moderation (finding). No logical relationship.
engine_resolution: Future research should empirically test whether Claim A's dynamic prompting approach improves outcomes uniformly across cultural groups, or whether Claim B's cultural moderation effect applies equally regardless of the underlying agent design principles.

explanation: Both studies report effects of simulation/practice-based training on psychological outcomes, but they measure genuinely different constructs: fear versus self-efficacy. Claim A shows that AI coaching reduced fear but did not build confidence (self-efficacy). Claim B shows that simulated practice reduced confidence (self-efficacy) even as it presumably addressed conflict skills. Together, these findings suggest that practice-based interventions can decouple anxiety reduction from confidence building — one goes down while the other plateaus or declines, revealing a more nuanced picture of learning effects.
claim_a_text: Trucey reduced negotiation fear most among all conditions, but produced only negligible improvement in organizational self-efficacy (delta=+0.05)
claim_b_text: Simulated conflict practice with Rehearsal reduced participants' self-efficacy after training as they confronted the real difficulty of conflict
claim_a_paper: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?
claim_b_paper: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution
gold_notes: Trucey and Rehearsal. Both find AI-assisted practice does not boost self-efficacy - Trucey negligible, Rehearsal a decrease. Same directional conclusion that AI practice does not build confidence.
engine_resolution: Future research should measure both fear and self-efficacy in the same study under identical conditions to clarify whether these are truly independent outcomes or whether one moderates the other, and whether the effect depends on feedback type, difficulty calibration, or population expertise.
